title: EMPACT Clinical Pearls & Study Hints
description: Key concepts, assessment tips, and clinical pearls from EMPACT Review for exam preparation (final micro-adjustments applied).

pearls.text: Differential Diagnosis — going from possibilities to probabilities.
Always identify and treat life threats first (Airway — Breathing — Circulation).
In the medical patient, about 80% of the information comes from the history and 20% from the physical examination.
Size-up: initial observations of the scene for safety, standard precautions, nature of illness, clues to the cause.
Primary Assessment: general impression, determine LOC, identify and treat life threats (A, B, C). May include suction, ventilation, oxygen. Determine patient priority.
Secondary assessment: history, physical examination, and vital signs.
Reassessment: ongoing assessment – more frequent if patient is unstable.
Pleural effusion is often associated with lung cancer.
The key to active listening is getting on your patient's level and asking follow‑up questions.
Indications for BVM include apnea, bradypnea, inadequate tidal volume, cyanosis, or altered mental status with poor respiratory effort.
Sepsis is frequently associated with fever and altered level of consciousness.
Wheezing is the hallmark sign of asthma.
Kussmaul respirations can be seen in diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) but not in HHNS or hypoglycemia.

Know the mnemonic CBIGK (Chest pain, Blood pressure, Irregular pulse, Glucose, Potassium) as a structured approach to evaluating potential cardiac and metabolic causes of chest pain and altered mental status.
If an ECG is non‑diagnostic, try to obtain a right‑sided ECG (to rule out right ventricular infarct).
Stridor indicates upper airway obstruction and is a potentially life-threatening finding requiring immediate airway assessment and intervention.
Cushing's Triad includes hypertension, bradycardia, and an altered respiratory pattern – indicative of increased intracranial pressure (ICP).
Pale skin, tachycardia, and tachypnea are signs of compensated shock. Altered mental status is the most reliable indicator of inadequate perfusion and decompensated shock.
JVD, muffled heart tones, and hypotension (Beck's Triad) indicate cardiac tamponade.
Hypoxia is a common problem with respiratory complaints.
HHNS (Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar Nonketotic Syndrome) is associated with undiagnosed Type II diabetes in which there is still pancreatic function (some insulin present).
Diabetic emergencies (hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia) can present with stroke‑like symptoms – always check a blood glucose.
Review stroke assessment protocol (e.g., FAST: Face, Arm, Speech, Time).
Keep shock patients warm by covering them with a blanket to prevent heat loss.
Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) that are altered because of illness is why people call EMS.
Always trust YOUR exam (your own primary assessment and findings).
Pulmonary embolism typically presents with sudden onset dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain (worse with inspiration), not typically reproducible with palpation.
If more than one person presents with the same signs and symptoms, you should inquire about other patients with the same or similar complaints (potential environmental/toxic exposure).
A common cause of sepsis in the elderly is pneumonia or UTI secondary to indwelling urinary catheters.
Nebulized beta-2 agonists are first-line for asthma not responding to home therapy. If the patient has altered mental status or inadequate respiratory effort, prioritize assisted ventilation (BVM).
Appendicitis often initially presents with periumbilical pain that transitions into right lower quadrant pain.
Patients with mediastinal cancer have a higher incidence of pleural effusion.
Anaphylaxis treatment: IM epinephrine is first-line. Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts and do not replace epinephrine in severe reactions.
NPA: used in patients with intact gag reflex or semi-conscious. Contraindicated in suspected basilar skull fracture or severe facial trauma. OPA: used in unresponsive patients without gag reflex.
Falling blood pressure is indicative of decompensated shock (progressive stage).
Hyperkalemia is relatively common in patients who miss dialysis and is associated with paresthesia (tingling) and palpitations (irregular heart rhythm).
Inotropic vasopressors (e.g., dopamine) are indicated for persistent hypotension in cardiogenic or septic shock after adequate fluid resuscitation.
When assessing a patient with a headache, a neurological exam should be performed to rule out stroke etiology.
Respiratory failure can be identified by a very slow respiratory rate (bradypnea) with shallow breathing or inadequate tidal volume.
During an exam of a conscious medical patient, ask questions first and do a physical exam second (history before physical).
Inferior MI: II, III, aVF. Anterior MI: V1–V4. Posterior MI: ST depression in V1–V4 or confirmation with posterior leads (V7–V9).
Hypotension and airway swelling (stridor, angioedema) are most diagnostic for anaphylaxis.
Meningitis is an infection and is associated with fever, nuchal rigidity, and altered mental status.
Indications of dehydration include skin tenting (poor turgor) and dry mucous membranes.
Hypoglycemia presents with altered mental status and requires immediate glucose administration. IV fluids may be considered if dehydration is present, but glucose is the priority.
Spontaneous pneumothorax commonly occurs in COPD patients and presents with sudden shortness of breath and sharp chest pain, often with unilateral decreased breath sounds.
Foreign body in the airway (e.g., vomit or blood): make sure you turn the patient on their side and suction to prevent aspiration.
